summary: Investigate whether a gen/HOAG module engine can keep nixpkgs compat but cut evalModules cost

result.costModel: RANKED by where a real NixOS/flake eval actually spends, eager-first. (A) EAGER PHASE-1 SHAPE CONSTRUCTION — the single largest unavoidable-on-any-read cost. `merged = mergeModules prefix (reverseList (doCollect {}).modules)` (modules.nix:275) is demanded by any read of config/options. It (i) imports every transitively-imported module file (`import m`, modules.nix:449-451) and resolves the full `imports` closure via genericClosure de-dup (564-573); (ii) canonicalizes each module (unifyModuleSyntax, 634-702, with removeAttrs over reserved keys); (iii) reflects every module function's formal-arg NAMES via functionArgs (applyModuleArgs, 724-732); (iv) builds the option/definition SHAPE — declsByName=zipAttrs(map .options) (789-808), pushedDownDefinitionsByName=zipAttrsWith concatLists(map .config) (831-852), pushing mkIf/mkMerge/mkOverride DOWN (pushDownProperties, 1335-1356) and calling unsafeGetAttrPos per option name. This is the O(#modules × #option-paths) transpose, eager in attrNames at every nesting level, independent of which config attr you read. (B) EAGER _module.check SHAPE-FORCE — default true; `checked = seq checkUnmatched` wraps options/config/_module (378,401-402); checkUnmatched (304-376) forces merged.unmatchedDefns = removeAttrs of the COMPLETE attrName set of options vs config at every level (927-956). A second full shape walk. Skipped by check=false or a freeformType (305) — a real tunable knob. (C) LAZY PHASE-2 PER-OPTION VALUE MERGE — scales with options actually demanded, not declared. evalOptionValue→mergeDefinitions runs dischargeProperties (mkIf eval)→filterOverrides' (priority)→sortProperties (mkOrder)→type.merge per demanded option (1100-1311), with an explicit fast path for the single-unwrapped-definition case (1214-1235) that most option reads hit. (D) LAZY-BUT-MULTIPLICATIVE SUBMODULE RECURSION — the dominant cost in a fully-forced real config. Every `attrsOf/listOf (submodule …)` runs ONE fresh independent evalModules per element via base.extendModules (types.nix:1452-1455 → modules.nix:380-393). systemd.services/sockets/timers/targets/paths and mounts/automounts are all `attrsOf/listOf (submodule …)` (nixos/lib/systemd-types.nix:131-227), so a system with N units pays ~N nested collect+merge passes; nesting compounds to O(N×M). mergeModules' even auto-wraps every bare dotted option subtree in a synthetic submoduleWith (855-871). Bounded by laziness to demanded paths, but `system.build.toplevel`/nixos-rebuild forces the product. (E) THE // COPY STORM — measured ~1.85M values-copied single-system, ~3.55M three-system (BENCHMARKS.md:36,49,62); each `//` copies all attr-POINTERS of both operands (pointers, not values). Concentrated in nixpkgs import + per-flake-input packages//legacyPackages processing (adios-flake lib.nix:231-238), NOT module dispatch. Quadratic under left-folded `//`; forcing the spine (attrNames/mapAttrs/toJSON) over a merged set defeats laziness and pays the full copy even without reading values. Eager-vs-lazy summary: SHAPE (A,B) is eager and proportional to declaration size; VALUES (C,D) are lazy and proportional to demand; the // storm (E) is eager-once-spine-forced and dominated by content not machinery.
result.intrinsicVsMachinery: This separation IS the feasibility answer. INTRINSIC (unavoidable while retaining nixpkgs content): (1) the // copy storm — BENCHMARKS.md shows adios CUTS it by only -0.3% (1,851,228→1,846,322) despite abandoning the module system entirely; these copies are nixpkgs import + flake-input packages//legacyPackages merges, paid by anyone who imports nixpkgs. (2) The final per-host option-merge fixpoint: two definitions of one option merging by type.merge with mkOverride/mkIf/mkOrder semantics has NO gen replacement (gen-algebra record.combine is left-biased scoped-label stacking, foldLayers is per-field-name strategy without mkIf/numeric-priority/type-driven submodule merge; gen-derive resolves WHICH rules fire, not how VALUES merge). If you keep nixpkgs option content you must run a real merge somewhere. (3) The import-closure forcing: `imports` are static-by-design (config-dependent imports are structurally forbidden, modules.nix:270 addErrorContext), so resolving the file graph is intrinsic to honoring nixpkgs modules. MACHINERY (potentially avoidable): the ~60K function-calls + ~50K prim-ops the BENCHMARKS.md analysis explicitly attributes to "evalModules, option type checking, and merge machinery" (lines 71-76) — translating to the measured -26%/-29% function-calls, -30%/-44%/-47% prim-ops, -36%/-38%/-40% attr-lookups, -20%/-27%/-28% thunks. Specifically avoidable: (a) the zipAttrs/mergeModules' per-path SHAPE transpose (A) when amortized across a graph of regions/flake-inputs that re-pay it under flake-parts' nested evalModules; (b) _module.check shape-forcing (B), skippable; (c) doc/description path (submoduleWith getSubOptions/extendModules for docs, types.nix:1434,1469-1474) when never demanded; (d) redundant re-collection of the SAME system-independent module prefix across N systems (adios-flake's headline win); (e) applyModuleArgs' per-module mapAttrs+`//` arg synthesis (724-739). SIZE OF THE AVOIDABLE SLICE — be precise and honest: the ONLY measured number is adios-flake's ~28-30% CPU on a FLAKE-OUTPUT workload (perSystem packages/formatter/devShells over 36 inputs × 3 systems), where the avoidable cost is the module-system machinery flake-parts pays PER-SYSTEM and adios shares once. This is NOT a measurement of a single nixosSystem's internal module cost; there, the dominant cost is intrinsic (D submodule recursion + the option merge itself), and adios buys its per-module fine-grained incrementality precisely by NOT hosting nixpkgs modules. CRITICAL inversion from the gen reports: gen-schema and gen-aspects are built ON the module system (base=lib.types.deferredModule entry-type.nix:32; introspection forces lib.evalModules entry-type.nix:223), so gen as-shipped ADDS machinery, it does not remove it. Therefore the avoidable slice for a compat-preserving framework is bounded to: (i) graph-plane composition cost moved OFF evalModules (the adios-flake ~30% class, realizable), plus (ii) cross-system/cross-input prefix sharing, plus (iii) skippable check/doc paths — NOT the intrinsic per-host merge or the // storm. The honest ceiling is "adios-flake-class speedup on the flake-output/multi-system axis while keeping nixpkgs leaves opaque," not "make a single host's evalModules cheap."

candidates.name: Memoize the system-independent module prefix across systems/inputs
candidates.thesis: The biggest MEASURED win (adios-flake's ~30%) is sharing the evaluation of modules that do not depend on the per-system axis, instead of re-running collect+merge once per system.
candidates.mechanism: Build ONE root evaluation, then for each additional system reuse already-forced thunks for every system-independent region, recomputing only the system-dependent cone. adios does this via firstTree.override {options={"/nixpkgs"=...}} reusing memoArgs=removeAttrs prevEval.args diff / memoResults (adios default.nix:417-490, adios-flake lib.nix:498-520); a HOAG analogue runs one lib.fix scope graph whose per-node _eval thunks (gen-scope/lib/eval.nix:24-53) are pointer-identical across systems for nodes that never read the system input. Crucially this is INTRA-eval thunk sharing (Q1: no cross-process memo exists in pure Nix), so the shared root and the per-system outputs must live in one eval.
candidates.expectedSaving: ~28-30% CPU on multi-system flake-output workloads (directly measured BENCHMARKS.md); near-zero on a single nixosSystem
candidates.risk: Only helps where a system-independent prefix EXISTS and is large (flake outputs, multi-system). For a single host build there is no axis to share over, so no win. Soundness requires that the shared region genuinely never reads the per-system input — a config-dependent read silently couples and stales the share.

candidates.name: Confine evalModules to terminal output leaves; run all composition in pure Nix (HOAG two-plane)
candidates.thesis: Pay the full module-system machinery ONCE per materialized host leaf, never amortized across the composition graph, by doing host/aspect/setting resolution off the module system.
candidates.mechanism: Run structure/aspect-resolution/settings-precedence on a gen-scope graph (lib.fix + co-located _eval memoization, eval.nix) using gen-algebra foldLayers for default<env<host<policy precedence (rec.nix:264) and gen-derive for activation; keep all intermediate composition off evalModules. Final resolved module fragments stay deferredModule (gen-aspects types.nix) and cross into ONE real lib.nixosSystem at the output-modules attribute (den v2 r2:800-815,952). nixpkgs modules hosted verbatim via gen-bind.wrapAll.
targets: redundant nested evalModules across composition steps (flake-parts-style)
candidates.expectedSaving: Eliminates the per-region evalModules tax (the adios-flake ~30% class) WITHOUT abandoning nixpkgs; the intrinsic single-host merge cost is unchanged
candidates.risk: Win depends entirely on HOW MUCH composition you move off evalModules. If little, little win. Behavioral parity of a real nixpkgs host routed through output-modules+gen-bind vs plain lib.nixosSystem is a DESIGN CLAIM, not yet store-hash-verified (r2 open question). gen-schema/gen-aspects themselves force evalModules on introspection (entry-type.nix:223) — must keep that to definition time only.

candidates.name: Partition the config fixpoint via explicit typed HOAG edges
candidates.thesis: NixOS cannot statically partition because every module reads one shared `config` fixpoint; explicit typed I-edges name exactly which region draws from which, enabling region-granular incremental override.
candidates.mechanism: Replace implicit any-reads-any coupling with HOAG P-edges (lexical context) and I-edges (computed from imports), specificity D
targets: the single flat option namespace at one evalModules level (non-partitionable today)
candidates.expectedSaving: O(|changed reverse-dependency cone|) per override instead of O(|tree|) — at REGION granularity, not per-option
candidates.risk: DECISIVE CAVEAT: incremental at the edge/region boundary, NOT inside a nixpkgs module. The moment an edge crosses INTO `config` (pipe.withConfig, r2:649-655) you re-couple both hosts' fixpoints and lose the laziness — the same coupling NixOS has, now explicit/opt-in. Granularity is structurally coarser than adios (per-host leaf vs per-module). Whether real configs can stay config-free at edges is unestablished (r2 Open-Q 2).

candidates.name: evalModules-lite: drop _module.check, doc-gen, rarely-used priority paths
candidates.thesis: A drop-in evaluator that retains nixpkgs option/merge SEMANTICS but skips the eager shape-forcing checks and documentation machinery the common path never needs.
candidates.mechanism: Set _module.check=false to skip checkUnmatched's full attrName-set shape walk (modules.nix:305,378); never force the submodule getSubOptions/description path (types.nix:1434,1469-1474); keep the mergeDefinitions fast path (1214-1235) and only fall back to the full dischargeProperties→filterOverrides→sortProperties pipeline when mkIf/mkOverride/mkOrder are actually present (already gated). Optionally a freeformType to bypass unmatched checks (305).
candidates.expectedSaving: Removes one full attrName-set shape walk (B) and all doc-path evalModules; modest on top of intrinsic merge — unmeasured here
candidates.risk: Dropping _module.check loses the definition-without-declaration error (a real correctness signal users rely on); typo'd options become silent. Partial-compat: any module/tool that demands generated docs or the unmatched check breaks. The priority pipeline is already lazily skipped, so the saving there is small.

candidates.name: Lazy-splice / persistent merge instead of left-folded // copies
candidates.thesis: The // storm is quadratic under left-folds and forced wholesale on spine access; a persistent/HAMT-style splice or right-leaning fold reduces pointer-copy volume.
candidates.mechanism: Replace cascading `acc // f x` folds (which recopy the whole accumulator each step, measured 51,275 pointer-copies for a 50-deep fold) with a single zipAttrsWith/listToAttrs pass or a structure that shares unchanged sub-bindings. Lazy-transpose the declsByName/pushedDownDefinitionsByName build so an option's value is computable without materializing the whole-tree transpose first (r2 open question).
candidates.expectedSaving: BENCHMARKS shows // is intrinsic to nixpkgs import (-0.3% even after dropping the module system) — so the realizable slice is ONLY the module-machinery folds, not the nixpkgs-import bulk. Small absolute win.
candidates.risk: The dominant // volume is nixpkgs import, unavoidable while keeping content (proven by adios's -0.3%). A lazy-transpose that lets you read one option without the full declsByName risks changing unmatched-check and freeformType semantics. Requires evaluator-level changes Nix doesn't expose at the language level for the import bulk.

candidates.name: Opt-in edge-annotated incremental modules with full-eval fallback
candidates.thesis: Let module authors optionally declare their cross-module read-set (adios-style `inputs`), unlocking adios-grade per-module incrementality for annotated modules while unannotated nixpkgs modules fall back to the opaque full-eval leaf.
candidates.mechanism: A two-tier engine: annotated modules carry an explicit typed-input edge (exact by contract, like adios inputs/types.nix:65-76) and get memoArgs/memoResults diff-reuse on override; unannotated nixpkgs modules are evaluated by the real evalModules as one opaque leaf (C2). The edge set is the diff oracle (default.nix:463-467).
candidates.expectedSaving: Per-module incrementality for the annotated fraction; nixpkgs fraction unchanged (still per-host leaf)
candidates.risk: Adios's diff soundness REQUIRES the declared edge set coincide with the actual data-dependency set; for nixpkgs modules reading the shared `config` implicitly, an annotation that under-declares silently stales the diff. Only sound for modules that genuinely route deps through explicit edges — i.e. exactly the modules that are NOT vanilla nixpkgs. The compat-preserving leaf gets ZERO incremental benefit, so this helps only NEW code.

candidates.name: External static read-set discovery by parsing .nix
candidates.thesis: Discover a module's config.* read-set without evaluating it, by parsing the AST and walking config-rooted selects, to drive region partitioning automatically.
candidates.mechanism: nix-instantiate --parse emits a structured AST (Lix 2.96); config.services.bar.enable appears as ExprSelect{attrs=[services,bar,enable],e=ExprVar config} (verified /tmp/q2parse.nix). An external pass over ExprSelect-on-ExprVar-config extracts a static over-approximation of the read-set per file.
targets: edge discovery for C3/C6 without manual annotation
candidates.expectedSaving: Enables automatic partitioning that would otherwise require per-module annotation
candidates.risk: UNSOUND under dynamism: computed names config.services.${key}.enable lose the leaf; `with config.services; foo.enable` defeats name resolution (ExprWith + unqualified foo); let-aliasing and passing config into helpers break the ExprVar-rooted walk. Must fail-open to the whole prefix (over-approximate), and any over-approximation that widens to 'reads everything' collapses partitioning back to the monolithic case. Lix-specific AST format is a portability concern.

candidates.name: Coarse-grain per-host / per-flake-input evalModules memoization
candidates.thesis: Treat each host's full evalModules (and each flake input's output set) as one cacheable unit, sharing the result across consumers that ask for different outputs of the same unit.
candidates.mechanism: Within one eval, hold each host's config fixpoint as a single shared thunk so multiple flake outputs reading the same host (e.g. toplevel + a doc + a test) force it once; mirror adios-flake's per-input mkInputsPrime sharing (lib.nix:231-238) so packages//legacyPackages of an input are merged once.
targets: redundant re-forcing of the same host/input across outputs
candidates.expectedSaving: Eliminates duplicate full-host evals when multiple outputs reference one host; part of the measured multi-system win
candidates.risk: Only helps when the same unit is referenced by multiple demanded outputs. Cross-PROCESS sharing is impossible in pure Nix (Q1) — confined to one eval invocation. Does nothing for the per-host internal cost.

candidates.name: Submodule-eval deferral / shared-base hoisting for attrsOf-submodule
candidates.thesis: The dominant intrinsic cost in real configs is N independent evalModules per attrsOf-submodule element; hoist the shared option-declaration resolution out of the per-element loop.
candidates.mechanism: submoduleWith already shares the construction-time `base` across elements of one type instance (types.nix:1393), but each element re-runs extendModules collect+merge (1452). Investigate parameterizing a single shared evalModules by element name so per-element work is only the value merge, not re-collection — the gen reports flag this as the key design question (whether gen's lazy splice can hoist shared option-decl resolution out of the element loop).
targets: submodule recursion multiplier (D) — the dominant real-config cost
candidates.expectedSaving: Potentially large on configs with many units (systemd.services etc.) IF the per-element re-collection (not just value merge) is a measurable fraction — UNMEASURED; needs NIX_SHOW_STATS partitioning of collect vs merge vs evalOptionValue
candidates.risk: This is the deepest intrinsic cost and the hardest to touch without changing submodule merge semantics (per-element mkIf/mkOverride must still work). May require evaluator changes. Whether re-collection vs value-merge dominates is an open question across reports — could be a no-op if value-merge dominates.

candidates.name: gen-derive as a rule-activation layer (NOT a mergeDefinitions replacement)
candidates.thesis: Use gen-derive to resolve WHICH aspects/overlays/policies apply to a host deterministically, but explicitly NOT to merge option VALUES.
candidates.mechanism: gen-derive dispatch (override→priority→exclusive→additive, topoSort phases, dispatch.nix) decides rule firing with deterministic tie-break (priority desc, decl-order); pair with foldLayers for scalar/record precedence and the terminal evalModules for true type-driven merge. Honest scoping: gen-derive is a rule engine, not an option-value merger.
candidates.expectedSaving: Replaces hand-rolled activation logic with a memoized fire-once dispatch; not a module-cost reduction per se
candidates.risk: REPORTS AGREE this does NOT replace mergeDefinitions — no gen lib reproduces type-driven submodule merge (confirmed gap). Mis-scoping it as a merge replacement would lose mkOverride/mkIf/type.merge semantics. Eager per-dispatch (foldl' forces all conditions), budget one per host not per option.

claim.claim: The // copy storm is INTRINSIC to nixpkgs content, not module machinery: abandoning the entire NixOS module system (adios-flake vs flake-parts) reduces values-copied-via-// by only -0.3% (1,851,228→1,846,322 single-system; 3,565,364→3,555,945 three-system), while function-calls drop -26%/-28%, prim-ops -30%/-44%, attr-lookups -36%/-38%.
claim.whyLoadBearing: Sets the hard ceiling on what a compat-preserving framework can save: the largest single metric (>1.8M copies) is unavoidable while importing nixpkgs, so the addressable slice is the ~60K function-calls/~50K prim-ops of merge machinery (BENCHMARKS.md:71-76), i.e. roughly the measured ~30% CPU on flake-output workloads, NOT a larger figure.

verdicts.nuance: Upheld. The copy storm is the nixpkgs package set fixpoint, not the module system. Caveats: content bound since disabling aliases drops copies about a sixth; the trigger is importing nixpkgs not forcing packages, so the ceiling applies only to package-forcing workloads which the benchmark targets; cross-system import memoization is a separate axis the claim ignores, weak since pkgsCross sharing backfires.
verdicts.evidence: Fixpoint folds lib.extends copying the full package set per layer; module system copy sites are small attrsets; importing only the lib subdir copies almost nothing; stdenv force dominates the benchmark total while pure evalModules copies little but calls many functions.

verdicts.nuance: Claim survives and is mechanistically VALIDATED by independent measurement, not merely consistent with the cited benchmark. The eval-semantics soundness rests on three facts I verified: (1) The counter is `nrOpUpdateValuesCopied` (Nix/Lix `eval.hh:1074`), incremented EXCLUSIVELY inside `ExprOpUpdate::eval` — it counts attribute-SLOT copies (key+thunk pointer, lazy in values) produced when a `//` result is forced to WHNF. It is NOT an abstract "merge" counter; it is tied to syntactic `//` over forced attrsets. (2) `//`-slot-copies and module-merge cost are nearly ORTHOGONAL cost centers, so eliminating the module system cannot move the `//` metric much by construction. A pure `evalModules` over 200 modules merging 10,000 values into an `attrsOf` option (no package set) produces only 683 `//` copies while doing 131,556 function calls / 66,588 prim-ops — because the module system merges via `zipAttrsWith`/type-driven recursion (`lib/modules.nix`), not via large `//` operations. Its overhead is exactly the function-calls/prim-ops/thunks that DO drop 26-44% in the benchmark. (3) The ~1.85M copies are genuinely intrinsic to nixpkgs CONTENT: they come from the package-set fixpoint, forced by the mere act of `import <nixpkgs> {}`. CONDITIONS/CAVEATS where care is needed: (a) The claim must be read as "the module-machinery-addressable slice is capped at ~30% (the function-call/prim-op merge cost), and the 1.75M-copy fixpoint is off-limits to any framework that imports nixpkgs and exposes its packages" — NOT as "// is irreducible in all senses." Because `//` copies are lazy slot-copies fired at WHNF, a framework that forced FEWER distinct package attrs could in principle reduce them — but that is an output-SELECTION effect, not a framework-ARCHITECTURE effect; both flake-parts and adios force identical outputs in the benchmark (confirmed by near-identical counts and the 1.926x linear-in-#-systems scaling, which tracks # of pkgs instances, not framework). (b) The numbers are honest and slightly conservative: the framework-attributable delta (4,906 single / 9,419 three-system = 0.265%) is precisely flake-parts' own small `//` usage inside its module machinery that adios drops. (c) For the PROJECT's goal: this BINDS HARDER in the project's favor — a HOAG/lazy compat-preserving framework cannot touch the 1.75M-copy `extends`/`composeExtensions` fixpoint either, so the realistic ceiling is the ~30% merge-machinery slice, exactly as the claim states.
verdicts.evidence: Counter semantics: Nix/Lix dev header `eval.hh:1074` declares `Counter nrOpUpdateValuesCopied;` adjacent to `nrOpUpdates` — printed as "Values copied (//)"; incremented only in ExprOpUpdate. Empirical decomposition (NIX_SHOW_STATS, Lix 2.96, NIX_PATH=~/Documents/repos/nixpkgs): `import lib` alone (no package set) = 494 // copies; `import <nixpkgs> { config={}; overlays=[]; }` forcing only attrNames = 1,756,268 // copies; adding a full `lib.evalModules` on top = +99 copies (1,756,364); forcing `hello` = +473 (1,756,738). So ~1.756M of the ~1.85M copies are the package-set fixpoint construction itself, before any output is forced. Source of the storm: `lib/fixed-points.nix:333` `extends = overlay: f: final: let prev = f final; in prev // overlay final prev` and `:344-346` `composeExtensions` (`prev // fApplied; fApplied // g final prev'`) — each overlay/stdenv-stage `//` copies the entire growing ~10K-attr top-level set; chained across bootstrap stages + overlays => millions of slot copies. Orthogonality proof: bench_mod_scale (200 modules, 10,000 merged values, NO pkgs) = 683 // copies vs 131,556 function calls / 66,588 prim-ops. Cross-workload confirmation: a full NixOS config eval (`nixos/lib/eval-config.nix`, module-system's home turf) = 1,835,456 // copies — only ~79K (+4.5%) above the bare `import <nixpkgs>` baseline of 1,756,268, despite nrOpUpdates rising 2,894→18,034 (those extra updates are over small definition/option attrsets, few slots each). adios genuinely avoids evalModules: `adios/default.nix` merges via its own `foldl'`+`//` (lines 171-184, 428, 486, 515), confirming the benchmark is module-system-vs-direct-dispatch, so the 26-28% function-call drop is real machinery elimination — and the -0.3% // result is the correct, honest consequence.

Reproduced (NIX_SHOW_STATS, no-eval-cache): import nixpkgs + force hello.drvPath = 2109565 copies / 74339 fcalls (one package exceeds the 1.85M total). import-only typeOf pkgs (zero packages) = 1756268 copies / 20316 fcalls (fixed floor approx benchmark). 200-option evalModules no packages = 2444 copies / 11250 fcalls (copy-light). lib alone=494; 10 packages=4375429 (content-linear). 0/5/20 empty overlays=identical 2109565. Mechanism per-package, framework-independent: fixed-points.nix:333,344,346; customisation.nix:171,314; make-derivation.nix:236,650 (file-top line 2 names // the hot cost). Non-amplification: flake-parts types lazyAttrsOf types.package (packages.nix:14, devShells.nix:15, checks.nix:14); types.package.merge=mergeOneOption (types.nix:625, options.nix:451) by reference. Both frameworks incur identical package copies; module removal moves only fcall/primop axis, matching BENCHMARKS.md:33-76.
verdicts.nuance:
Survives strong attack; reproduced (Lix 2.96, nixpkgs 2f4f625e). Module system fcall/primop-dense but copy-sparse (~12 copies/option); package set inverse, so removing evalModules cuts fcalls/primops and barely touches copies, matching -26%/-0.3%. Refinement to wording not conclusion: the ~1.76M floor is intrinsic to importing+forcing nixpkgs derivations and scales with packages referenced, not a fixed constant of nixpkgs content. But both frameworks touch the same packages via lazyAttrsOf types.package by reference, so module system cannot change package copies. Addressable slice correctly bounded to merge machinery (~30% CPU), not the >1.8M copies.</nuance>
<parameter name="claimId">H1-sentinel

claim.claim: The only MEASURED speedup (~28-30% CPU) is on FLAKE-OUTPUT/multi-system workloads (perSystem packages/formatter/devShells over 36 inputs × 3 systems), where flake-parts re-pays module machinery per-system; there is NO measurement of reducing a single nixosSystem's internal evalModules cost, whose dominant cost is the intrinsic per-host option merge plus attrsOf-submodule recursion.
claim.whyLoadBearing: Falsifiable scope limit: the feasibility verdict is 'adios-flake-class win on the composition/multi-system axis,' not 'cheaper per-host module eval.' Conflating the two would overclaim. Falsified if a benchmark shows a single nixosSystem getting comparable speedup from a compat-preserving driver.

verdicts.nuance: Claim survives a direct source attack. Both clauses check out against actual source. (1) The "only measured speedup is flake-output/multi-system" clause: adios-flake/BENCHMARKS.md:18-63 benchmarks exactly and only `packages.aarch64-darwin`, `formatter` (3 systems), `devShells` (3 systems) over the 36-input Mic92/dotfiles flake — three perSystem categories, zero nixosConfigurations rows. The ~28-30% CPU figure is correctly attributed (BENCHMARKS.md:65-76) to eliminating flake-parts' per-system module machinery, confirmed by the near-zero delta in values-copied (-0.3%, dominated by nixpkgs import, NOT the framework). (2) The "no reduction of single nixosSystem internal evalModules" clause is structurally guaranteed, not merely unmeasured: adios-flake never builds a NixOS system. `nixosConfigurations` is a flake-SCOPED passthrough output (lib.nix:48-58, defaultFlakeOutputs), merged/transposed as an opaque value — adios-flake's own tests prove this by assigning literal strings/scalars to it (tests/collisions.nix:92 `nixosConfigurations.myhost = "myhost-config"`; tests/flake-outputs.nix:71-82; tests/integration.nix:183 returns a plain attrset). The real `lib.nixosSystem` call lives inside the user's module impl, hitting nixos/lib/eval-config.nix → lib.evalModules over baseModules = module-list.nix (2047 entries), entirely outside adios-flake's machinery. (3) The "dominant cost = intrinsic per-host option merge + attrsOf-submodule recursion" characterization is faithful to source: evalModules eagerly runs collectModules over ALL regularModules and mergeModules' builds declsByName (a zipAttrs over every option decl in every module) before any config value is read (modules.nix:261,275,785-808) — the declaration/merge tree is NOT lazy even though option VALUES are; and every attrsOf-submodule instance triggers a NESTED evalModules via submoduleWith.merge.v2 → base.extendModules (types.nix:1393,1449-1455), so users.users.*/systemd.services.*/fileSystems.* each re-pay submodule eval per entry. adios's own engine (korora-typed, adios/default.nix) never calls evalModules at all, so it cannot and does not touch this cost. Caveat where it could be overstated but is NOT, because of careful wording: the claim asserts a MEASUREMENT/scope limit ("there is NO measurement"), not an impossibility theorem. A future compat-preserving driver could in principle attack per-host cost (e.g. by memoizing the base-module declaration tree across hosts, since the 2047-module merge skeleton is host-invariant). The claim does not deny this; it only states no such measurement exists today, which is true. Falsification condition (a benchmark showing a single nixosSystem getting comparable speedup from a compat-preserving driver) is unmet — no such benchmark exists in either repo.

WHY adios cannot reach per-host eval: adios (adios default.nix 44-316) is a SEPARATE module system with its own computeOptions/evalModuleTree and never calls nixpkgs evalModules; adios-flake only reimplements the flake-parts API on it. nixosConfigurations (adios-flake lib.nix 49) is a passthrough flake-scoped collector; withSystem (lib.nix 398-407) only injects pkgs/system/inputs-prime/self-prime via intersectAttrs into a user function whose nixpkgs.lib.nixosSystem call is opaque. Tests confirm passthrough: collisions.nix 92 stores literal host string, flake-outputs.nix 76 a bare system string, never a module-set eval. WHAT WAS MEASURED: BENCHMARKS.md 18-23 only packages.aarch64-darwin, formatter (3 sys), devShells (3 sys) over 36 inputs x 3 systems, no host. BENCHMARKS.md 36 and 72-76: attrset-copy count flat at minus 0.3 percent while function calls drop 26-29 percent and prim ops 30-47 percent, attributed in-prose to NixOS evalModules, type-checking, and merge machinery flake-parts re-pays per system; copies dominated by nixpkgs import. INTRINSIC PER-HOST COST the claim names: nixpkgs types.nix 902 attrsWith runs mergeDefinitions per key over elemType; types.nix 1393 submoduleWith runs a FRESH evalModules per submodule instance; modules.nix 785-923 mergeModules recursion; 77 attrsOf-submodule option sites in nixos modules, each instantiated entry paying a full option-merge cascade. BOUNDARY/NUANCE that could be misread as overstatement: the minus 29 percent single-system packages number measures flake-parts perSystem ASSEMBLY overhead, not a single-HOST config.system.build.toplevel; home-manager and clan-core content in the benchmark flake is collected as passthrough output attrsets, never evaluated through adios. So the composition/multi-system axis vs per-host module-eval distinction holds exactly. Claim only falsifiable by a benchmark showing a compat-preserving driver cutting a real nixosSystem toplevel eval, which adios architecture cannot produce because retaining nixpkgs modules means retaining their evalModules.

claim.claim: A single evalModules level has a FLAT, non-statically-partitionable option namespace because every module reads the shared `config` fixpoint and declsByName/rawDefinitionsByName zip ALL modules' keys together (modules.nix:789-852); the ONLY clean partition boundary that exists today is the submodule, which is already an independent evalModules (types.nix:1452).
claim.whyLoadBearing: Determines that HOAG-style partitioning (C3) must introduce EXPLICIT typed edges to gain region-granularity, and that this granularity is structurally coarser (per-host/per-region) than adios's per-module — falsified if one could show implicit config reads are statically recoverable for arbitrary nixpkgs modules.

verdicts.nuance: The claim survives. The flat config namespace is non partitionable and the clean boundary is the submodule.
verdicts.evidence: The flat namespace zips all module keys per name. The shared config fixpoint reaches every module. Only formal argument names are statically known, never which config path the body reads. Implicit and dynamic config reads make the read set undecidable. The submodule is an independent evalModules.

modules.nix:789 declsByName=zipAttrs(map m.options); :844-852 rawDefinitionsByName=zipAttrs(map m.config) -> flat union namespace; :707-739 applyModuleArgs builds extraArgs only over functionArgs f (732) then f (args//extraArgs) (739), passing the single global config; :728-729 dynamic config._module.args.${name} indirection; :386-393 extendModules re-evals regularModules++modules (superset, not partition). types.nix:1393 submoduleWith base=evalModules (disjoint fixpoint); :1452 per-def base.extendModules. nixos/modules/services/web-servers/nginx/default.nix:1685 reads config.boot.kernelPackages from services.nginx (real cross-namespace read). Empirical nix eval: functionArgs({...}:..)={} hides ellipsis config reads; a module `{...}: {config.bar=args.config.foo+1;}` yields bar=43 with functionArgs introspect={}; merged options namespace = ["_module" "a" "b" "c"].
verdicts.nuance: Survives strongest attack. (1) FLAT namespace TRUE: zipAttrs flattens all modules' option/config keys by name; empirically options = single union ["_module" a b c]. (2) Shared config TRUE and stronger: applyModuleArgs passes the one global config to every module; the WHY-IT-MATTERS falsifier (implicit reads statically recoverable) is empirically FALSE because applyModuleArgs enumerates only functionArgs f, so ellipsis/whole-set modules read cross-namespace config (e.g. nginx -> config.boot.kernelPackages) while functionArgs returns {}. Reads NOT statically recoverable for arbitrary nixpkgs modules -> confirms, not refutes. (3) Submodule = only clean partition TRUE: submoduleWith base=evalModules uses only the submodule's own modules (disjoint fixpoint); correction-only: extendModules/specialisation are NOT counterexamples since they re-eval regularModules++modules (superset sharing parent namespace), which the claim correctly excludes. Caveat: H3 describes today's mechanism, not theoretical impossibility -- a bespoke AST analyzer could approximate read-sets but cannot be sound (dynamic args.${name}/config._module.args.${name} indirection + ellipsis capture) and none exists in nixpkgs. So for an engine consuming unmodified nixpkgs modules, partition granularity is structurally bounded by the submodule (per-region), coarser than adios per-module. H3's downstream conclusion holds.

Upheld on its load-bearing core; two wordings are imprecise but non-falsifying. The falsification condition named in WHY-IT-MATTERS ("implicit config reads are statically recoverable for arbitrary nixpkgs modules") is FALSE in the implementation, so the claim survives. (1) IMPRECISE: "flat" — the option namespace is actually a recursive TREE (mergeModules' recurses per-name, resultsByName re-enters mergeModules' at modules.nix:922), not flat. But tree structure yields no module-partition: any module may contribute to any node, so the operative property "non-statically-partitionable into module-owned regions" holds; read "non-partitionable" for "flat". (2) UNDERSTATED: "ONLY the submodule" — other independent-evalModules boundaries exist: extendModules (modules.nix:386), specialisation (nixos/.../specialisation.nix:60, itself via extendModules), and the whole submoduleWith type family (attrsOf submodule, lazyAttrsOf submodule, deferredModule). But every one of these IS a nested evalModules, so the claim's category ("an independent evalModules") is correct; it merely under-enumerates entry points. Each remains a whole sub-evaluation containing MANY modules — strictly coarser than adios's per-module, which reinforces (not refutes) the structural-coarseness conclusion C3/coarser-than-adios. CONDITIONS under which the claim could weaken: a HOAG engine could in principle wrap each nixpkgs module in an instrumented config proxy that records accessed paths at eval time (dynamic, not static, dependency capture), recovering finer-than-region granularity without explicit edges — but that is runtime tracing, not the static recoverability the claim denies, and pays its own overhead; it does not falsify the static-partition claim.
verdicts.evidence: Core mechanism (non-static reads): every module is applied as args@{config,...}: out where out == module.config is an arbitrary function of the WHOLE shared config fixpoint. config built at modules.nix:279-302 (declaredConfig = mapAttrsRecursiveCond over options), fed back into doCollect as the `config` arg at modules.nix:270, distributed to every module via applyModuleArgs (modules.nix:707-739); args a module doesn't take explicitly are pulled from config._module.args.${name} (modules.nix:727-729) — itself a config read. The only static per-module record (unifyModuleSyntax, modules.nix:673-702) is {_file,_class,key,disabledModules,imports,options,config}; config is an opaque attrset, and functionArgs f (modules.nix:732) sees only the top-level `config` formal — NO record of which config.x.y paths were read. config.x.y is an ordinary Nix attr-selection invisible to static analysis. Zip citation accurate: declsByName = zipAttrs(...module.options...) (modules.nix:789-808) and rawDefinitionsByName = zipAttrs(...module.config...) (modules.nix:844-852) key by option-name across ALL modules with no provenance-partition retained; mergeModules feeds m.config at 781/840/850. Submodule-boundary citation accurate: types.nix:1452 base.extendModules → evalModules (lib.modules.evalModules inherited at types.nix:1375; extendModules→evalModules at modules.nix:386). Comparative anchor (adios buys per-module via EXPLICIT typed edges): adios modules declare typed inputs with .path (adios/default.nix:253-255), closure via genericClosure over inputs (resolveTree 245-256), each module reads only args.inputs/args.results of its DECLARED inputs (273-283) — never a shared config; per-module memoization (memoArgs/memoResults) and sub-closure invalidation via diff (453-469, mkOverride). This is precisely the explicit-edges-grant-granularity mechanism the claim says HOAG would need, and the absence of any such edges (only zipped name-keyed contributions + opaque whole-config reads) in nixpkgs is why a single evalModules level is not statically partitionable below the nested-evalModules boundary.

claim.claim: A module's config.* read-set cannot be discovered by pure Nix without evaluating it (functionArgs exposes only formal NAMES, no body reflection); external AST parsing recovers an over-approximation that is UNSOUND under `with`, dynamic selects (config.services.${key}), and config-aliasing, so automatic edge discovery must fail-open to the whole prefix.
claim.whyLoadBearing: Falsifies any design that promises EXACT automatic partitioning of vanilla nixpkgs modules; partitioning must be either contract-based (explicit declared inputs/edges, exact-by-construction) or a conservative over-approximation — and over-approximation that widens to 'reads everything' collapses back to the monolith.

verdicts.nuance: UPHELD. functionArgs gives formal names only, no body reflection, so config reads need external AST parsing, which is unsound under with config, dynamic select, and config aliasing, all present in nixpkgs source.

verdicts.nuance: UPHELD on its core mechanics; one sub-clause is materially overstated. The three load-bearing pieces all verify against source. (1) functionArgs is name-only with zero body reflection: `builtins.functionArgs ({config,pkgs,...}: config.services.nginx.enable)` evaluates to `{ config = false; pkgs = false; }` — and on the catch-all pattern `args@{...}: args.config...` it returns `{ }`, seeing nothing. nixpkgs has 3 module files using the `name@{...}` catch-all where even the arg NAME is invisible. adios itself relies on exactly this: adios/adios/default.nix:41 `callFunction = fn: attrs: fn (intersectAttrs (functionArgs fn) attrs)` passes only named formals, which is WHY adios requires explicit structural `inputs`/`impl` declarations (default.nix:167-168, 255, 273) — the contract-based, exact-by-construction path the claim's WHY-section names. (2) Dynamic selects are real and value-dependent: 54 occurrences of `config.X.${...}` in nixos/modules; the canonical kimai.nix:14 `webserver = config.services.${cfg.webserver}` where `cfg.webserver = config.services.kimai.webserver` is a config value, plus movim.nix:628, zabbix.nix:282, users-groups.nix:1207 `config.programs.${shell}.enable`. I reproduced the soundness defeat in evalModules: `options.dyn.default = config.${config.sel}` reads `config.a` only because `sel="a"` — any config-aliasing module redefining `sel` silently relocates the read-set, so no pre-eval static analysis can recover it soundly. (3) Config-aliasing is structural, not incidental: doRename (modules.nix:2028-2061, backing mkRenamedOptionModule/mkAliasOptionModule) installs `apply = x: use (toOf config)` plus `mkAliasAndWrapDefsWithPriority (setAttrByPath to) fromOpt` — reading `from` forces `to`, defining `from` writes `to`, and the alias module (e.g. rename.nix) is SEPARATE from the consumer, so the from↔to edge is invisible to any per-module AST scan. CONCLUSION: exact automatic partitioning of vanilla nixpkgs modules by pure-Nix read-set discovery is impossible; partitioning must be contract-based or conservative — confirmed.
verdicts.evidence: REFUTATION ATTEMPT that the claim must absorb (the one overstatement): the claim's terminal phrase "external AST parsing... UNSOUND under `with` [so it] must fail-open to the whole prefix" overstates the blast radius. There is NO bare `with config;` anywhere in nixpkgs nixos/modules or pkgs (grep `with config;` = 0 hits). All 53 `with config.X` occurrences scope to a KNOWN prefix subtree: searx.nix:316 `with config.services.nginx.virtualHosts.${cfg.domain}`, iwd.nix:21 `with config.networking.networkmanager`, netbird/coturn.nix:77,84,155 `with config.services.coturn`. So `with` widens an AST over-approximation to a bounded prefix (e.g. config.services.nginx.virtualHosts.*), NOT to the entire `config`. That means "fail-open to the whole prefix" collapses partition GRANULARITY within that subtree, not partition EXISTENCE — the engine still cleaves the rest of config cleanly. The claim is therefore correct that automatic discovery is unsound and must fail-open, but its implied catastrophe ("widens to 'reads everything' collapses back to the monolith") is the WORST-case hypothetical, not the empirical nixpkgs reality where the conservative widening is prefix-local. HOWEVER this does not refute H4: even bounded over-approximation is still an over-approximation (not exact), and the dynamic-select + config-aliasing cases (kimai.nix:14, doRename modules.nix:2043/2057) defeat soundness regardless of `with`, because the read TARGET is a runtime value, not a prefix that AST parsing can even name. So exact automatic edge discovery genuinely fails; the design consequence H4 asserts holds. Net: refuted=false (claim survives), with the single nuance that the `with`-specific fail-open is prefix-bounded in practice, while the soundness kill is carried by dynamic selects and cross-module aliasing, not by `with`. Relevant files: ~/Documents/repos/nixpkgs/lib/modules.nix:704-739,1959-2062; ~/Documents/repos/nixpkgs/nixos/modules/services/web-apps/kimai.nix:11,14; .../movim.nix:628; .../config/users-groups.nix:1207; ~/Documents/repos/adios/adios/default.nix:41,167-168,255,273.

claim.claim: gen-schema and gen-aspects are built ON the NixOS module system (base = lib.types.deferredModule, entry-type.nix:32; introspection forces lib.evalModules, entry-type.nix:223; aspectType uses lib.types.mkOptionType/submodule/functionTo/deferredModule), so gen as-shipped ADDS module-system machinery rather than removing it; no gen primitive reproduces type-driven submodule option merge.

The correct architecture confines evalModules to terminal leaves (C2) and uses gen ONLY for the off-module graph plane; falsified only if gen-schema can be configured to introspect without evalModules, which the code does not currently support.

verdicts.nuance:
Claim UPHELD on its core and load-bearing assertions, with one bounded overstatement that does not meet the stated falsification condition. Verified against the actual shipped sources (gen-schema rev c072f76, /nix/store/6p3b71s0sqvzh9jl9shc3cin23l0wbhq-source; gen-aspects rev 146389e, /nix/store/ii1vfkmdwyb76618qq8hfqnw9v776z9n-source). The line numbers in the claim are from a slightly different commit but every cited construct is present and structurally identical.

WHAT IS CONFIRMED (every clause): (1) base = lib.types.deferredModule — entry-type.nix:32, exact. (2) Introspection forces lib.evalModules — entry-type.nix:221-223 (`dummy = lib.evalModules { modules = [ merged ]; }`), the ONLY way to populate .options/.refs (line 241). There is exactly ONE evalModules call site and NO config flag to introspect options/refs without it (grep for skip/lazy/noIntrospect: none). (3) aspectType uses mkOptionType (types.nix:44), functionTo (types.nix:78), deferredModule (types.nix:109), submodule (types.nix:113). (4) gen as-shipped ADDS module-system machinery — self-documented: gen-schema README:5 'Built on the NixOS module system'; README:118 the documented entry point is lib.evalModules. (5) 'no gen primitive reproduces type-driven submodule option merge' — TRUE: gen-schema delegates ALL option merge to deferredModule.merge (entry-type.nix:217) and evalModules; gen-aspects' own aspectSubmodule (types.nix:101-157) is lib.types.submodule with freeformType. No pure-gen option-merge primitive exists.

FALSIFICATION CONDITION NOT MET: 'falsified only if gen-schema can be configured to introspect without evalModules.' It cannot. The mkType custom-entry path (entry-type.nix:121-141, the path gen-aspects itself takes — schema.nix:20) does skip evalModules, but it does so by hardwiring options = {}; refs = {} (lines 137-139): you get EMPTY introspection, not evalModules-free introspection. That is C2 (confine evalModules to the consuming/leaf evaluation via __functor/imports), which is exactly the architecture the claim's WHY-MATTERS prescribes — it does not refute the claim.

THE ONE OVERSTATEMENT (immaterial to the verdict): The claim implies the whole graph/off-module plane needs evalModules. Empirically the PARENT-edge / collection plane does NOT. Collection extraction (parent, methods, validators, user collections) is a pure foldl' over raw defs run pre-merge (entry-type.nix:89-97). Calling the raw mkSchemaEntryType.merge directly and forcing .parent fires no evalModules trace (test3), whereas forcing .refs fires EVALMODULES-FORCED and even crashes on the unevaluated ref type (test4). So a HOAG graph plane built only on parent/collection topology is achievable module-system-free — but the moment you need ref edges or option introspection (the I-edges, the type-driven plane), evalModules is mandatory. The claim scopes its evalModules assertion precisely to introspection (options/refs), which is exactly where it holds; it merely over-implies about the parent plane, which it does not actually cite as needing evalModules.
verdicts.evidence: gen-schema entry-type.nix:32 `base = lib.types.deferredModule;`. entry-type.nix:221-223 `introspect = let dummy = lib.evalModules { modules = [ merged ]; };` — sole evalModules call, gating .options/.refs (line 241 `inherit (introspect) options refs;`). entry-type.nix:137-139 mkType path zeroes introspection: `options = { }; refs = { }; refinements = { };`. entry-type.nix:89-97 pure collection extraction (foldl' over defs, no module system). entry-type.nix:217 `merged = base.merge loc (strippedDefs ++ injected);` — option merge delegated to deferredModule. entry-type.nix:351 `refs = config.${fromKind}.refs;` — ref-edge graph plane sourced from introspect→evalModules. gen-aspects types.nix:44 `lib.types.mkOptionType`, :78 `lib.types.functionTo (aspectSubmodule cnf)`, :109 `lib.types.deferredModule`, :113 `lib.types.submodule`. gen-aspects schema.nix:20 mkType path (returns __functor value, defers evalModules to consuming NixOS/HM eval = C2). gen-schema README:5 'Built on the NixOS module system'; README:118 documented lib.evalModules entry; README:1022 mkType 'replaces the standard deferredModule merge'. EMPIRICAL (instrumented lib.evalModules with a trace, /tmp/gen_eval_test{2,3,4}.nix): forcing _edges parent OR ref plane via mkSchemaOption → EVALMODULES-FORCED fires (submodule wrapper). Raw mkSchemaEntryType.merge: forcing .parent → NO trace (collection plane is module-free); forcing .refs → EVALMODULES-FORCED + 'cannot convert a function to JSON' (ref introspection genuinely requires evalModules). No grep hit for any introspection-skip flag in entry-type.nix.

verdicts.nuance: Claim UPHELD on source. Every cited fact verified exact, and the two load-bearing parts both survive an adversarial attack. (A) "gen as-shipped ADDS module-system machinery": gen-schema's entry type IS the module system — base=lib.types.deferredModule (gen-schema/nix/lib/entry-type.nix:32), and the SOLE introspection path producing .options/.refs (consumed by codec.nix:40, docs.nix:13) is dummy=lib.evalModules{modules=[merged];} (entry-type.nix:223). gen-aspects' aspectType is lib.types.mkOptionType (lib/types.nix:44) dispatching to functionTo (78,94)/submodule (113,162)/deferredModule (109). These are module-system core, not standalone: evalModules lives at nixpkgs/lib/modules.nix:92, mkOptionType at lib/types.nix:187, and deferredModule's own getSubOptions/getSubModules are inherited from submoduleWith{...} (nixpkgs/lib/types.nix:1304-1307) whose body runs base=evalModules{...} (types.nix:1393) — so even merely introspecting a deferredModule's sub-options forces evalModules. The deferredModule.merge itself (types.nix:1297-1303) is trivial ({imports=...;}); the real type-driven option merge happens inside evalModules. The falsification condition (gen-schema configurable to introspect WITHOUT evalModules) is NOT met: there is exactly one evalModules call and no alternate path — confirmed by grep. The single genuine attack is on (B) "no gen primitive reproduces type-driven submodule option merge": gen-algebra/pure/rec.nix foldLayers/foldNestedLayers (lines 179-370) DO implement standalone, module-free, ordered-layer, per-field cascade with replace/append/recursive strategies — a real layered-merge primitive with zero module-system dependency. But this does NOT refute (B) as worded: foldLayers is a VALUE-level cascade over plain attrsets — it has no option types, no mkOption/freeformType separation, no priority algebra (mkForce/mkOverride/mkDefault/mkMerge), no type-driven submodule recursion, and no _module.args/name/config fixpoint. The claim's qualifier "type-driven submodule option merge" is precise and load-bearing; foldLayers reproduces the cascade SHAPE but not the type-system semantics, so (B) holds. Architecturally this CONFIRMS the C2 strategy the WHY-IT-MATTERS describes — and the den-architecture papers independently frame it identically: deferredModule is "the dimension boundary... where content transitions into a single dimension's evaluation context (NixOS evalModules)" (tarr-1999 summary:59) and "class content as terminal attributes... forced only at the output boundary" (lorenzen-2025 summary:84); gen-derive/lib/core/*.nix and gen-bind operate entirely off the module plane (graph rewriting / arg-collision linking, Cardelli framing), using NO lib.types. CONDITION under which the claim would FAIL: if one weakens "type-driven submodule option merge" to merely "layered field merge," foldLayers is a counterexample and (B) becomes overstated. As written, with the type-driven qualifier intact, the claim is accurate and not materially overstated.

claim.claim: Memoization that helps is strictly INTRA-evaluation thunk sharing (one lib.fix / one override-threaded root in one process); pure Nix has no primitive to persist or import a forced thunk across separate `nix eval` invocations, so 'adios incremental override' is intra-eval reuse of already-allocated thunks (memoArgs=removeAttrs prevEval.args diff), not a cross-process memo cache.
claim.whyLoadBearing: Bounds every memoization candidate (C1,C6,C8) to a single eval; a framework cannot amortize host evals across separate CLI invocations in pure Nix — falsified only by an out-of-language store/IFD cache, which leaves the pure-framework scope.

verdicts.evidence: adios override is pure intra-eval thunk reuse. File ~/Documents/repos/adios/adios/default.nix line 417 mkOverride = root: prevEval closes over in-heap prevEval; line 475 memoArgs = removeAttrs prevEval.args diff; line 476 memoResults = removeAttrs prevEval.results diff; removeAttrs shares thunk objects rather than copying. The override path imports only pure builtins at lines 11-30 (genericClosure, removeAttrs, mapAttrs, filter) and contains zero import, toFile, readFile, fetchTree, or getFlake. The override chain is exposed only as a returned attribute at lines 488 and 517. Docs corroborate intra-eval scope: adios-flake README line 29 evaluated once regardless of how many systems, docs/api-reference.md line 32 memoized across all systems, BENCHMARKS.md line 12 eval cache cleared between runs, adios doc/src/motivation.md lines 19-20 target high memory usage. Empirical eval-cache test: a locked flake re-prints trace FORCE-INNER on every separate nix eval process, no suppression. The eval-cache-v5 sqlite schema is parent, name, type, value, context; nixosConfigurations and config are stored as type 0 Unknown forcing re-eval on descent, only terminal scalars store a value such as leaf type 8 value 704982704 re-injected as a fresh value, never a live thunk. Serialization boundary: toFile then import round-trip is pure and re-imports 4999950000 without re-folding, but toJSON of a function fails with cannot convert a function to JSON and toString of a function fails with cannot coerce a function to a string. The full builtins enumeration on Lix 2.96 contains no thunk-serialization primitive.
verdicts.nuance: Claim survives. The word thunk is load-bearing and correct: no Nix builtin persists a forced thunk (heap object with identity and sharing). Two refutation candidates were chased to ground and both fail. The flake eval-cache does not memoize forcing across processes (verified: trace re-prints every run; near-all attributes stored as type 0 Unknown, re-evaluated on descent). The toFile-to-import round-trip CAN move a fully-forced DATA value across separate evals purely, but it crosses as a serialized scalar re-parsed into a FRESH thunk (identity and sharing lost), and functions are non-serializable, so the function-dense evaluated NixOS module graph cannot be persisted at all. A materially weaker claim that no value can ever cross processes purely would be FALSE, but H6 does not assert that and explicitly scopes the out-of-language store/IFD cache out, which is precisely the only escape. The bound on C1/C6/C8 (a pure framework cannot amortize a host eval across separate CLI invocations) holds. One honesty caveat: adios motivation.md line 19 phrasing no memoisation from a previous one reads ambiguously cross-eval, but line 20 high memory usage plus the README/api-reference framing confirm the real target is intra-eval branch sharing, matching H6.

Flake eval cache ~/.cache/nix/eval-cache-v5/*.sqlite schema Attributes(parent,name,type,value,context): forced leaf strings type=2, drvPath=/nix/store/7m3k9mg3...nixidy-activation-environment-prod-axon.drv with store-path context, outputsToInstall type=7; fixed taxonomy {0,1,2,3,7}, flake-locked-keyed, bare --expr has no applicable cache.
verdicts.nuance: Upheld on its own carve-out; the unqualified universal is overstated. In the pure-Nix sense (language, no IFD, no evaluator-tool feature) it is exactly correct: builtins exposes no cache/memo/persist primitive, and functions are non-serializable, so no forced thunk/closure crosses processes in-language. adios is characterized precisely: mkOverride returns override as a closure over the live in-process evalParams, and memoArgs/memoResults reuse already-allocated heap attrsets (removeAttrs prevEval.args/results diff) — pure intra-eval thunk reuse, no store/IFD/builtins; adios-flake re-imports fresh per eval. Two overstated edges, both pre-conceded by the WHY clause: (a) IFD is in-language and persists a forced RESULT via a store path import across evals (costs a build, leaves pure scope); (b) the Nix/Lix flake eval cache IS a cross-process forced-value store (verified on disk: leaf strings, drvPaths, outputsToInstall keyed by locked-rev+attrpath). Neither refutes H6 for a pure framework: the eval cache has no builtins handle, is flake-locked-keyed (not bare --expr), and caches only flake-output leaf values in a fixed shallow taxonomy — never an intermediate thunk, lib.fix fixpoint cell, or closure. A framework cannot author memoize-this-host-eval-and-reuse-across-CLI-calls. Net: the load-bearing conclusion (C1/C6/C8 bounded to one eval; no in-language cross-invocation amortization) holds; only the absolute wording is loose, rescued by the explicit out-of-language exception.

claim.claim: Retaining nixpkgs option CONTENT forces a real type-driven merge (mkOverride priority + mkIf + mkOrder + type.merge submodule recursion) to run somewhere, because no gen primitive replicates it (gen-algebra foldLayers is per-field-name strategy without conditionals/numeric-priority/type-merge; gen-derive resolves rule firing not value merge); therefore the intrinsic per-host merge cost cannot be eliminated, only relocated to a single terminal evalModules and shared/deferred around.
claim.whyLoadBearing: This is the core honest constraint separating the avoidable machinery (composition/check/doc/prefix) from the unavoidable intrinsic (the merge itself); falsified only by exhibiting a gen-based merger that is byte-identical to lib/types submodule merge under mkOverride/mkIf/mkOrder.

verdicts.nuance: UPHELD against attack, with one material scoping nuance the study should adopt. The claim holds at the level of *semantics*: nixpkgs's value merge is priority-driven and position-INDEPENDENT (mkOverride numeric-min via filterOverrides', mkOrder numeric sort, mkIf conditional discharge that can make a def evaporate to the default, and type.merge recursion that re-enters evalModules for submodule/attrsOf). No gen primitive replicates this. I empirically proved byte-divergence on the claim's own named falsification test: with IDENTICAL layer order [mkForce["c"], mkDefault["z"], normal["n"]], nixpkgs yields ["c"] (force wins despite being FIRST), while gen-algebra foldLayers replace yields ["n"] (last positional) and append yields the concatenation — and NO reordering of foldLayers layers can fix this because foldLayers is priority-blind and position-driven while nixpkgs is the inverse. mkIf false yields the default ["base"], which foldLayers cannot model without the consumer pre-discharging the condition (i.e., relocating, not eliminating, the cost — exactly the claim). adios CONFIRMS the premise from the other side: adios deliberately does NOT retain these semantics — its computeOptions (adios/default.nix:44-111) picks exactly one source per option (passedArg>default>defaultFunc, no multi-def) and mergeOptionsUnchecked (178-199) is plain lhs//rhs last-wins recursive update == foldLayers. That is precisely the smaller-system replacement the proposed framework refuses, so retaining content does force the heavier merge. gen-derive's 'priority' (rule.nix:11, dispatch.nix:81) and gen-aspects' layer 'priority' are NOT value-merge priority — gen-derive selects which RULES fire (its own README: 'Actions are opaque -- the vocabulary belongs to the consumer'), and gen-aspects layers settings positionally; neither does mkOverride-style numeric-min over option VALUES. THE NUANCE / where the claim is materially OVERSTATED if read as 'every per-option merge is expensive': for the dominant single-definition, no-property leaf case, type.merge is a genuine no-op — mergeEqualOption/mergeDefaultOption/mergeOneOption all short-circuit to (head defs).value (options.nix:427-428,480-481,506-507) and mergeDefinitions has a fast path that skips discharge/filter/sort entirely (modules.nix:1221-1235). So the INTRINSIC cost is not 'a real type-driven merge' uniformly; it is (a) head-extraction (≈free, foldLayers-replicable) for unconflicted scalar leaves, plus (b) the unavoidable option-TREE WALK / nested-evalModules fixpoint for every typed submodule/attrsOf node (types.nix:1006-1033, 1449-1461), which runs regardless of def count, plus (c) the genuinely non-trivial priority/order/conditional/type-merge algebra ONLY for multiply-defined or property-wrapped options. The honest restatement: the irreducible kernel is the typed option-tree fixpoint walk + the merge algebra at conflict sites; that kernel cannot be replicated by gen primitives and so can only be relocated to a terminal evalModules and shared/deferred — NOT that each option pays a heavy merge. Under that restatement the claim's load-bearing conclusion (cost relocatable not eliminable; falsifiable only by a gen merger byte-identical to lib/types under mkOverride/mkIf/mkOrder) stands unrefuted.

EMPIRICAL (nix-instantiate --eval --strict): /tmp/reorder.nix mkForce-first → nixpkgs=[\"c\"] vs foldLayers-replace-same-order=[\"n\"] (falsification test fails to byte-match); /tmp/mkif.nix mkIf false → nixpkgs=[\"base\"] (def evaporates, default survives, foldLayers cannot model); /tmp/fold_probe.nix foldLayers append=[\"a\" \"z\" \"b\" \"c\"], replace=[\"c\"] vs nixpkgs full-priority eval=[\"c\"] (coincidental match only when force is last).

verdicts.nuance: Claim's CORE survives: no gen primitive replicates the nixpkgs merge, and the natural counterexample (adios) confirms rather than refutes. But the universal quantifier ("intrinsic per-host merge cost cannot be eliminated") is MATERIALLY OVERSTATED and must be narrowed on two eval-semantics grounds. (1) Single-definition fast path: nixpkgs modules.nix:1221-1240 skips the ENTIRE discharge/filterOverrides/sort/merge pipeline when length defs==1 and the value carries no _type wrapper — handing the singleton straight to type.check. For the majority of options (defined exactly once) NO priority/order/conditional merge machinery runs at all, so a gen engine can soundly bypass mkOverride/mkIf/mkOrder for single-def options. (2) Laziness: mergedValue sits behind lazy thunks (optionalValue/mergedValue, modules.nix:1310), forced only on access — unaccessed options pay zero. So the genuinely unavoidable residue is narrower than 'a real type-driven merge per host': it is 'the MULTI-definition / property-wrapped / submodule merge, on ACCESSED options, must run somewhere.' Within that narrowed scope the claim is correct and unrefutable: foldLayers cannot be byte-identical to lib/types submodule merge, and adios only achieves zero cost by discarding the content the claim's antecedent requires retaining. Verdict: upheld with narrowing, not falsified.
verdicts.evidence: gen-algebra foldLayers (pure/rec.nix, store 3ijk5l0w...:178-215): per-field-name dispatch to replace|append|recursive ONLY. replace=head of contributions (insertion order, NOT priority); append=list ++ fold; recursive=builtins.foldl' (acc: l: acc // l.${name}) — flat shallow // update, no type recursion, no numeric priority, no mkIf discharge, no mkOrder sort, no file-tracking for conflicts; contributions filtered by plain `l ? ${name}` membership. nixpkgs mergeDefinitions (lib/modules.nix:1185-1311) runs dischargeProperties(mkIf/mkMerge)->filterOverrides'(numeric mkOverride min-priority, 1415-1436; mkForce=50/mkDefault=1000/mkOptionDefault=1500, 1567-1571)->sortProperties(mkOrder numeric sort, 1449-1465)->type.merge. submoduleWith.merge (lib/types.nix:1445-1453) = base.extendModules{modules=...++allModules defs} i.e. a RECURSIVE evalModules per submodule occurrence; attrsOf/lazyAttrsOf v2 merge (types.nix:789-848) does per-element peelProperties unwinding order+override. None replicated by foldLayers. gen-derive (flake desc 'stratified rule dispatch with fixpoint convergence') resolves rule FIRING not value merge — matches claim. REFUTATION ATTEMPT via adios FAILS: adios/korora types (types/types.nix) expose ONLY verify/check, NO merge fn anywhere; adios computeOptions (adios/default.nix:44-111) is single-assignment (passedArg XOR default XOR defaultFunc XOR recurse, never a defs list); mergeOptionsUnchecked (178-199) is bare lhs // rhs last-wins shallow. Adios pays zero merge cost precisely by DISCARDING nixpkgs option content/semantics — exactly the antecedent ('Retaining nixpkgs option CONTENT') the claim excludes. No den-spec prior analysis of this terminal-evalModules irreducibility found (grep parity/specs empty for the claim).

verdicts.nuance: UPHELD with two caveats. (1) Slight overstatement: "cannot be eliminated, only relocated" is true for the per-host-input-dependent FLOOR, but nixpkgs has real fast paths (modules.nix:1221-1235 singleton no-property bypass; filterOverrides' singleton case 1424-1428) that lower the per-option constant when an option has exactly one wrapper-free definition. A HOAG engine maximizing single-definition options pays strictly LESS intrinsic merge than naive evalModules — floor is lower than "full machinery," but never zero while nixpkgs content is retained (evalOptionValue ALWAYS prepends mkOptionDefault opt.default at modules.nix:1104-1112, so most options have >=2 defs and skip the fast path). (2) gen-derive genuinely HAS numeric priority + overrides + exclusive (dispatch.nix:81,69-73,86); the claim's parenthetical survives only because that priority gates which RULE FIRES (which produce-fn runs), not how produced values combine into a typed value — a subtle but correct distinction. The claim holds STRICTLY because it is conditioned on "retaining nixpkgs option CONTENT": the merge is unavoidable for that content, only relocatable/shareable/deferrable.
verdicts.evidence: foldLayers char. is EXACT: gen-algebra pure/rec.nix:179-214 — strategies are per-field-NAME static {replace|append|recursive}, recursive=shallow // (line 205), positional last-wins (line 201), NO numeric priority, NO conditional inclusion, NO type-recursion. gen-derive is rule-firing not value-merge: lib/core/dispatch.nix:16-142 — priority/overrides/exclusive select firing rules -> action lists grouped by phase (line 138); extract/combine caller-supplied, identity by default (line 120,27). No other gen primitive replicates merge: gen-aspects/lib/schema.nix:61,64,81,102 DELEGATES to lib.mkOption/lib.types.submodule/lazyAttrsOf — it is built ON nixpkgs merge, not a replacement. The four irreducible nixpkgs mechanisms: filterOverrides' (modules.nix:1415-1436) numeric-priority winner-take-all, order-independent (no foldLayers analogue); dischargeProperties (1375-1385) mkIf/mkMerge discharge (no analogue); sortProperties (1449-1465) priority-keyed stable sort (no analogue); submodule.merge.v2 (types.nix:1445-1461) calls base.extendModules => full recursive evalModules, and attrsOf.merge.v2 (1006-1034) zipAttrsWith mergeDefinitions per key — recursive re-eval that // cannot express. STRONGEST attack = adios, which CONFIRMS the premise: adios/adios/default.nix computeOptions (44-111) picks ONE value/option (passed>default>defaultFunc>nested, no def-list/priority/mkIf/mkOrder); mergeOptionsUnchecked (179-199) = lhs // rhs + sub-option recursion; korora types (adios/types/types.nix) have 0 merge / 42 verify. Adios drops the merge ONLY by abandoning nixpkgs content for a single-valued option model — so it is the existence proof that merge is the unavoidable cost OF retained content, not a counterexample. Precompute is no escape: 1692 nixos modules gate on mkIf cfg.enable (config-fixpoint-dependent), so dischargeProperties must run inside the per-host fixpoint, not as host-independent constant.
